lens.included_activities: U.S. multi-employee, facility-based pet boarding, daycare, and grooming operators, including businesses combining those services with supervised exercise, feeding, sanitation, monitoring, and non-veterinary ancillary care.
lens.excluded_activities: Veterinary services; pet retail and manufacturing; animal shelters; solo or primarily mobile pet sitting, dog walking, and training; captive services; and firms outside the lower-middle-market operating-company population.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer-paid boarding nights, daycare visits or packages, grooming appointments, memberships, and ancillary services delivered from staffed facilities, with repeat use and local customer relationships but generally limited contractual commitment.
lens.deviation_from_default: The official code combines facility-based boarding, daycare, and grooming with solo or mobile sitting, walking, and training. The lens is narrowed to multi-employee facility-based operators solely to obtain a coherent asset, staffing, customer, and transfer model; the excluded activities have materially different economics and operating obligations.

a.bridge: BLS reports animal care and service occupations as the dominant occupational group in the broader NAICS 812900 category, and its occupation handbook details feeding, cleaning, exercising, grooming, behavioral monitoring, and basic care tasks. I treat booking, intake, records, reminders, customer communication, marketing, scheduling, and limited monitoring/reporting as the automatable share. Because NAICS 812900 includes unrelated personal services and the handbook includes self-employed and veterinary-context workers, the proxy can bias task shares in either direction; narrowing to facilities likely lowers the share relative to solo service businesses.
a.rationale: The selected operators primarily sell supervised physical care of living animals in a cleaned, staffed facility. AI and software can reduce administrative work and improve scheduling, capacity planning, communication, intake review, and routine observation, but feeding, cleaning, handling, exercise, grooming, and welfare response remain physical and context-sensitive.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study of facility-based boarding, daycare, and grooming employees, separating care, cleaning, customer, supervisory, and administrative tasks and measuring feasible automation, would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupational industry table covers broader NAICS 812900 rather than the exact code or narrowed lens.
Task feasibility does not establish adoption, safe unattended use around animals, or realized labor reduction.

rho.rationale: Scheduling, payments, customer messaging, digital intake, vaccination records, cameras, and facility-management systems provide a foundation for incremental AI. Realization is limited by fragmented small operators, uneven data, customer privacy, safety and liability, staff adoption, integration cost, and the need for human review when animal health or behavior may be involved.
rho.change_evidence: Representative facility-operator evidence on deployed AI functions, integration costs, staff utilization, incident rates, customer acceptance, and realized hours or revenue effects would materially change the estimate.
caveats: No representative exact-code AI adoption and realization study was located.
The estimate assumes ordinary business software exists but does not treat basic digitization as incremental AI realization.

e.rationale: Facility-based boarding, daycare, and grooming can form transferable operating companies with staff, leases or real estate, equipment, customer records, and repeat revenue. The exact code is substantially broader, however, and includes solo, mobile, reputation-dependent, or part-time services; even within the narrowed group, small scale, owner dependence, lease restrictions, weak records, and local permitting can prevent an entity from being a practical stand-alone acquisition.
e.change_evidence: Exact-code establishment microdata segmented by facility status, employment, revenue, owner dependence, service mix, lease transferability, and independent ownership would materially change the range.
caveats: Public exact-code data do not cleanly separate the narrowed facility-based segment.
Customer goodwill may attach to individual groomers or founders rather than the business entity.

s5.bridge: Gallup finds that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners intended to sell or transfer their company within five years. I use this cross-industry intention rate as an upper anchor and reduce it for completed market availability, since stated plans may become family transfers, closures, or no transaction. Facility assets, repeat customers, and staff aid transfer, while founder reputation and lease or permit constraints justify the discount; the national sample may misstate this industry's owner age and consolidation in either direction.
s5.rationale: A staffed facility can be sold as a going concern, but only a minority of owners will complete a sale or control transfer within five years. Groomer or founder relationships, animal-care reputation, premises, licensing, buyer financing, and local consolidation can prevent intended transitions from becoming actionable opportunities.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year exact-code record of completed sales, family transfers, closures, withdrawn processes, and ownership changes for facility-based operators would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The proxy is cross-industry and measures intentions, not completed transactions.
Small private transactions and transfers are often not publicly reported.

q.rationale: Appointment prices, packages, memberships, and differentiated service can let an operator retain improvements as capacity, convenience, response speed, or quality rather than immediately lowering price. Capture is constrained by local competition, price-sensitive households, high direct-care labor needs, customer expectations for staffing and safety, software cost, and the limited administrative share to which AI applies.
q.change_evidence: Operator-level evidence linking technology deployment to labor hours, occupancy, ancillary sales, pricing, churn, incidents, customer satisfaction, and vendor cost would materially change the range.
caveats: No exact-segment evidence directly measures retention of AI-enabled economics.
Boarding, daycare, and grooming differ in capacity constraints, pricing, and labor intensity.

d5.bridge: BLS projects animal care and service worker employment to grow 11% from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to pet ownership and household treatment of pets as family. BLS also projects broader NAICS 812900 real output to grow from $97.0 billion in 2024 to $127.6 billion in 2034, about 2.8% annually. I translate those decade paths into a conservative five-year multiplier between the slower employment signal and faster broad-industry output signal. The occupation and industry aggregates include activities outside the narrowed segment and may overstate or understate facility pet-care demand.
d5.rationale: Repeat boarding, daycare, and grooming demand is supported by pet ownership, travel and work patterns, and willingness to pay for care. It remains discretionary for many households and can weaken with consumer budgets, remote work, price increases, substitute care by friends or family, or shifts toward lower-frequency services, so the central case is moderate rather than exceptional real growth.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted revenue, visits, occupied boarding nights, daycare utilization, grooming frequency, openings and closures, and customer-cohort retention for the narrowed segment would materially change the multiplier.
caveats: Neither BLS series isolates facility-based boarding, daycare, and grooming.
Employment growth, real output, and unit demand are related but not interchangeable.

o.rationale: Boarding and daycare require a responsible operator to house, supervise, feed, clean, exercise, monitor, and respond to living animals, while grooming requires physical handling and skilled service. Cameras, automated feeders, self-service washing, informal caregivers, and in-home alternatives can substitute for portions of the service, but they do not eliminate accountable physical care for most facility use cases.
o.change_evidence: Customer substitution data by service line, including informal care, in-home platforms, self-service grooming, automated monitoring, cancellation reasons, and incident or regulatory requirements, would materially change the range.
caveats: The strength of the obligation differs between overnight boarding, routine daycare, and elective grooming.
A high value reflects the persistence of physical care, not nondiscretionary spending or guaranteed customer frequency.

narrative.executive_view: The narrowed segment is a repeat local service built around staffed facilities and the accountable physical care of animals. Its operating obligation is durable, but the amount of work susceptible to incremental AI is modest and concentrated in administration, planning, communication, and observation rather than direct care.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term uses include booking and capacity management, intake and record review, reminders, customer messaging, marketing, staff scheduling, report generation, and triage of camera or sensor alerts. Feeding, cleaning, exercise, animal handling, grooming, behavioral judgment, and emergency response remain human and physical, with safety and liability limiting unattended automation.
narrative.value_capture: Packages, memberships, appointments, and service differentiation can preserve some gains as capacity, convenience, or quality. Local competition and household price sensitivity constrain pricing, while high direct-care labor and staffing expectations mean administrative efficiencies affect only part of the cost base.
narrative.firm_availability: Facility operators can possess transferable staff, systems, customer records, equipment, and premises rights, but the NAICS code also contains many solo and mobile businesses that do not share that model. Even after narrowing, founder or groomer dependence, leases, permitting, reputation, and incomplete records reduce the population of actionable stand-alone companies.
narrative.demand_durability: Pet-care employment and broader personal-service output projections support continued demand, and pet owners need trusted care during travel, work, and routine grooming cycles. Demand remains exposed to household budgets, remote-work patterns, informal substitutes, service frequency, and local capacity additions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The central uncertainty is segmentation: public evidence rarely separates facility boarding, daycare, and grooming from sitting, walking, training, self-employment, or unrelated personal services. Other uncertainties include customer discretionary spending, local regulation and leases, staff availability, animal-safety liability, owner or groomer dependence, and the absence of representative AI realization data.
narrative.principal_driver: The need for trusted, accountable physical care of animals in repeat local relationships.
narrative.principal_weakness: A heterogeneous industry population and a relatively small administrative task share limit both eligible firm coverage and the scale of incremental automation.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 812910 Pet Care (except Veterinary) Services
sources.supports: Defines the exact code as boarding, grooming, sitting, walking, and training services for pets other than veterinary care, establishing its heterogeneous activity mix.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 812900
sources.supports: Shows animal care and service workers as a large share of employment in the broader other-personal-services industry.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Animal Care and Service Workers
sources.supports: Details physical care duties, work settings, injury and scheduling conditions, and projected 2024-2034 employment growth for animal care and service workers.

sources.title: BLS industry employment and output projections
sources.supports: Reports projected 2024-2034 employment and real output for the broader NAICS 812900 industry.

sources.title: Gallup: Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports national employer-business-owner intentions to sell or transfer within five years and the gap in succession preparation.
